title: Exponents with integer bases
description: Practice raising positive and negative numbers (integers only) to whole number powers. Watch out for mischievous negative signs that aren't really part of the base!

hints:
We can use what we know about integer multiplication to evaluate the expression.

A negative sign is only part of the base of an exponent if it is inside parentheses. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For example:

$\begin{align}
\bigstar^2 &= \bigstar \cdot \bigstar
\\\\
(-\bigstar)^2 &= (-\bigstar) \cdot (-\bigstar)
\\\\
-\bigstar^2 &= -1 \cdot \bigstar \cdot \bigstar
\end{align}$

The base of the power is $-8$.

$\begin{align} &(-8)^2\\\\
=& (-8) \cdot (-8)
\end{align}$

We can use what we know about integer multiplication to evaluate the expression.

A negative sign is only part of the base of an exponent if it is inside parentheses. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For example:

$\begin{align}
\bigstar^2 &= \bigstar \cdot \bigstar
\\\\
(-\bigstar)^2 &= (-\bigstar) \cdot (-\bigstar)
\\\\
-\bigstar^2 &= -1 \cdot \bigstar \cdot \bigstar
\end{align}$

The base of the power is $-3$.

$\begin{align} & (-3)^4\\\\
=& (-3)\cdot(-3)\cdot(-3)\cdot(-3)
\end{align}$

We can use what we know about integer multiplication to evaluate the expression.
